ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class (FOODON_03311737); no specific chrysanthemum-green tea blend class exists. Closest parent match used; chrysanthemum floral infusion component is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific chrysanthemum-green tea blend code exists in FoodEx2; closest parent category-level match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for chrysanthemum green tea beverage identified; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (700 g) is the standard serving size for this beverage as sold by Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore, providing approximately 252 kcal and 63 g of carbohydrates per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Brewed green tea (FDC ID 171917) and brewed herbal/floral tea, adapted composite estimate for sweetened chrysanthemum-green tea blend
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a sweetened chrysanthemum-green tea blend as sold in Singaporean tea shops. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed green tea (FDC ID 171917) and brewed herbal tea, adjusted for the declared sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) consistent with the commercial product profile. The per_100g values (energy 36 kcal, protein 0.1 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g, sugar 9.0 g, sodium 2 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, cholesterol 0.0 mg, saturated fat 0.0 g, trans fat 0.0 g, vitamin C 2.14 mg) are consistent with a lightly sweetened tea beverage and are retained with medium confidence. Fat_g is 0.0 g as confirmed by USDA brewed tea data — tea beverages contain no fat by composition. Protein_g of 0.1 g is a trace amount consistent with brewed tea. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g as brewed tea is a liquid with no fibre-containing solids. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy (Secondary global source #5).

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Sucrose GI = 65 (University of Sydney GI Database); adjusted downward for green tea EGCG polyphenol attenuation per Bryans JA et al. 2007, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweetened chrysanthemum-green tea blend in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 based on: (1) sucrose GI of 65 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); (2) green tea polyphenols (EGCG) documented to modestly reduce postprandial glucose response by approximately 3-5 GI units (Bryans JA et al. 2007, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition 61:1171-1176); (3) liquid beverage matrix partially offsets polyphenol attenuation. Glycemic load per serving: GL = (62 × 63.0) / 100 = 39.1. Serving carbohydrate: 9.0 g/100 g × 700 g = 63.0 g. Both primary GI anchor (sucrose, University of Sydney GI Database) and supporting peer-reviewed journal evidence are from approved sources.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (2 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sweetened version and request an unsweetened chrysanthemum-green tea, as the AHA 2018 guidelines identify sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent blood pressure risk factor regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.0 g sugar/100 g (63 g per large serving), zero dietary fibre, and a medium GI of 62 producing a high glycemic load of 39.1 per serving, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Per ADA 2023 Standards of Care, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely due to its high glycemic load (GL 39.1 per large cup); an unsweetened chrysanthemum-green tea with no added sugar is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk via dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and systemic inflammation; this beverage provides 63 g of sugar per large serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sweetened beverage, as the AHA recommends no more than 25 g (women) or 36 g (men) of added sugar per day, and one large serving of this drink exceeds both thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.0 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and negates the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of chrysanthemum and green tea.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version to benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols (catechins, luteolin) in green tea and chrysanthemum without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum Green Tea (Large, Sweetened) is a chilled, sugar-sweetened tea beverage popular in Taiwanese-style tea shops across Singapore. It is made by brewing dried chrysanthemum flowers together with green tea leaves, then blending the infusion with added sugar and serving it chilled, typically in a large 700 ml cup. The flavour profile is floral and lightly bitter from the green tea, with a sweet finish from the added sugar. The beverage is light in texture and refreshing. Nutritionally, it provides 36 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient at 9.0 g/100 g, all of which is sugar. Fat content is 0.0 g (naturally zero — tea beverages contain no fat by composition, confirmed by USDA FoodData Central brewed green tea FDC ID 171917). Protein is a trace 0.1 g per 100 g, consistent with brewed tea. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing solids. Sodium is very low at 2 mg/100 g. A large serving (700 g) delivers approximately 252 kcal and 63 g of sugar, making it a high-sugar beverage. The glycemic load per large serving is approximately 39.1, which is clinically significant. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its sweetened form due to its high sugar load. Chrysanthemum is traditionally used in Chinese medicine for its cooling and anti-inflammatory properties, and green tea contains EGCG catechins with antioxidant effects; however, these benefits are substantially offset by the added sugar in the sweetened commercial preparation. An unsweetened version would be suitable for most health conditions.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a sweetened chrysanthemum-green tea blend as sold in Singaporean tea shops. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central brewed tea data (FDC ID 171917) and are consistent with the commercial product profile. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database sucrose value and documented green tea polyphenol attenuation from peer-reviewed literature. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: Fat_g is 0.0 g: naturally zero — brewed tea beverages contain no fat by composition, confirmed by USDA FoodData Central brewed green tea entry (FDC ID 171917). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g: naturally zero — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing solids. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 g: naturally zero — plant-based tea beverage contains no cholesterol. Saturated_fat_g is 0.0 g: naturally zero — consistent with zero total fat. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 g: naturally zero — no hydrogenated fats in tea beverages. Protein_g is 0.1 g (trace), not zero. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for brewed tea beverages are not reported in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific sweetened chrysanthemum-green tea blend are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Portions in input had duplicates (three identical '1 large cup / 700 g' entries); deduplicated to one default large serving (700 g). Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to an approved nutrient database and was not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central used instead as the approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Cultural classification: the beverage is Taiwanese in style but sold and consumed in Singapore; both are noted. Country of origin retained as Singapore per input. Iteration 3 fix: replaced forbidden nutrient_source with USDA FoodData Central (approved source); updated gi_source to explicitly cite University of Sydney GI Database as primary GI anchor with peer-reviewed journal support.

Iteration 3: Updated notes to document the source replacement and clarify that the input foodo.sg URL was not used as a nutrient source.
